text: Semi-disposable instruments are introduced directly through the skin without the use of a trocar, visible in the laparoscopic view.

The surgeon introduces a percutaneous instrument directly through the skin into the abdominal cavity, extracts it through an accessory port, and assembles it with a 5mm end effector, specifically an atraumatic Joann forceps.
The surgeon uses percutaneous instruments introduced directly into the abdominal cavity, minimizing trauma to the abdominal wall while performing the laparoscopic left colectomy.
The surgeon uses a percutaneous instrument to lift the sigmoid and descending colon upwards, exposing the root of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon uses monopolar energy to open the peritoneum of the sigmoid colon, exposing the root of the superior rectal artery.
The surgeon traces the path of the superior rectal artery as it leads to the exposed root of the inferior mesenteric artery, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin, then clips and divides the artery using surgical instruments.
The surgeon continues the procedure by dissecting the Toldt fascia, revealing underlying structures and maintaining clear exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection laterally and medially, approaching the root of the inferior mesenteric vein, with surrounding tissue being carefully separated.
The surgeon clips and divides the inferior mesenteric vein at the level of the tail of the pancreas, completing the vascular dissection in this area.
The surgeon dissects the tail of the pancreas from the root of the transverse mesocolon, entering the lesser sac.
The surgeon divides the lateral attachments of the sigmoid and descending colon using monopolar electrocautery, connecting this dissection with the previously performed medial dissection.
The surgeon completes the normalization of the splenic flexure by dividing the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the anatomical separation between the stomach and colon.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure and proceeds to divide the superior mesorectum, making incisions laterally on the left side, and both posteriorly and laterally on the right side.
The surgeon interrupts the mesorectum using harmonic energy and divides the superior rectum with an endoscopic articulated linear stapler, showing precise tissue manipulation and division.
The surgeon introduces a spatula into the vagina and performs a posterior colpotomy using monopolar electrocautery, visibly incising the tissue.
A standard wound protector is placed to protect the vagina, and the surgical specimen is extracted transvaginally.
The surgeon divides the mesentery of the descending colon and checks the perfusion of the colon using ICG fluorescence, confirming adequate blood flow at the proximal division site.
The surgeon divides the descending colon and introduces the anvil of the surgical circular stapler into the surgical field.
The surgeon reintroduces the specimen into the abdominal cavity and closes the wound protector using an appropriate closure system.
Inside the abdomen, the surgeon performs a standard end-to-end circular anastomosis, connecting the divided sections of the colon.
The surgeon performs an air leak test to check the integrity of the anastomosis, observing for any signs of air escaping from the surgical site.
The surgeon removes the wound protector from the vaginal opening and closes the colpotomy with a continuous suture using a transvaginal approach.
The surgeon removes the end effector of the percutaneous instruments from the surgical site.
The final view of the patient's abdomen is shown after completing a laparoscopic left hemicolectomy, with all surgical instruments removed and the incision sites closed.
